articles.title: Why Janet? (2023)
articles.summary: Janet, a Lisp dialect with imperative syntax and first-class functions, is an attractive language for developers due to its simplicity, ease of use, and high-level abstractions. Its small codebase and macros provide powerful control flow, while the runtime semantics are familiar to JavaScript users. The language's distributability and ability to compile into native executables make it suitable for writing command-line apps.

articles.title: You Don't Love Systemd Timers Enough
articles.summary: You don't love systemd timers enough because they offer utility that's almost immediately obvious for many use cases, such as scheduling daily and monthly tasks, but are often misunderstood or replaced by cron jobs due to their modern design and functionality. A systemd timer is a type of unit that schedules other units on a particular schedule, providing a functional replacement for the traditional cron daemon. Its ambiguous $PATH settings make it difficult to predict script execution, while stdout and stderr output can end up in black holes.

articles.title: kelvin versioning
articles.summary: Software versioning using Kelvin degrees is a system where each version must be cooler than the one it supports, or at absolute zero conjointly with it. This means that if tool B sits on platform A, both A and B must be at absolute zero to avoid conflicts. New versions of software must also follow this constraint, and any changes to an existing version must not exceed a certain threshold.

articles.title: Browser identification through header order
articles.summary: Browser identification through header order is a passive method that mimics browser detection code, but most browsers do not use the same header order as their genuine article counterparts. This can be useful for storing and verifying HTTP user agent strings, but it can also be exploited by malicious actors to intercept sensitive information. Modern browsers are more secure than older ones, making this technique less effective in practice.

articles.title: strace-ui, Bonsai_term, and the TUI renaissance
articles.summary: strace-ui, Bonsai_term, and the TUI renaissance is a new tool that has emerged to make debugging easier by turning strace into an interactive terminal UI. This tool assigns short IDs to PIDs, formats structs, and renders buffers as hexdumps instead of strings. It also features interactive filtering, tracing async OCaml processes, and more. Developed by Ian Henry in 2017 as a personal project after years of searching for such a tool, strace-ui is now part of the Bonsai framework, which provides a powerful reactive UI library for building web applications with OCaml.

articles.title: The postmodern build system
articles.summary: The postmodern build system aims to move incrementalism to a distrusting layer, making it harder for bugs to exist without computational waste. This approach would involve sandboxing jobs and rerunning them when necessary, but at the cost of increased computation in production use cases. It also lacks "build identity" which can lead to problems with multitenancy and reuse of computation.

articles.title: Should you normalize RGB values by 255 or 256?
articles.summary: Standard division by 255 maps integer values to floating-point values with an assumed range of [0.0,1.0], while alternative division by 256 adds a bias and divides by 256, resulting in a wider range that can make it harder to output extreme values from algorithms. The standard approach is preferred due to its simplicity and consistency with how GPUs handle integer-to-float conversions.
